title: BIT Manipulation Knowledge Test
description: Test your understanding of BIT Manipulation with 25 questions.

questions.question: Implement permute32x4_128(uint128 x) that reorders the four 32-bit lanes inside a 128-bit value: interpret x as [A,B,C,D] with A = high 32 bits of x.hi, B = low 32 bits of x.hi, C = high 32 bits of x.lo, D = low 32 bits of x.lo. Return a uint128 with lanes in the order [B,A,D,C] using only shifts and masks; no loops or conditionals. Signature: uint128 permute32x4_128(uint128 x); Provide the signature, a brief justification, and 1–2 quick tests?

questions.question: In a 32-bit header for a simplified protocol, two 16-bit fields are packed as [high16 | low16]. Write a function swapHalves32 that swaps the halves to produce [low16 | high16], using only shifts and masks (no branches or loops). Signature: uint32_t swapHalves32(uint32_t x); Provide the signature, a brief justification, and 1–2 quick tests?

Implementation: uint32_t swapHalves32(uint32_t x) { return (x >> 16) | (x << 16); } Reason: constant-time bitwise ops; no branches; portable across compilers. Tests: 0x12345678 -> 0x56781234; 0xAABBCC

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests familiarity with basic bit manipulation and endianness handling in a realistic, atomic operation.

## Key Concepts

- 32-bit width handling
- Bit shifts and OR composition
- Endianness considerations

## Code Example

```c
uint32_t swapHalves32(uint32_t x) {
  return (x >> 16) | (x << 16);
}
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you generalize to 64-bit words?
- How would you implement with explicit masks for clarity and portability?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

The question checks ability to design a constant-time, endianness-agnostic byte reversal on 128-bit data using only masks and shifts.

## Key Concepts

- Byte-level manipulation
- Masking and staged swaps
- Endianness independence
- Constant-time composition

## Code Example

```c
typedef struct { uint64_t hi; uint64_t lo; } uint128;
uint128 reverseBytes128(uint128 x) { /* staged swaps with masks */ }
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you generalize to 256-bit types?
- How would you test across different endianness architectures?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

This question tests understanding of parity computation and branchless reduction techniques—skills essential in cryptography (e.g., Hamming code validation, checksums) and error-detection routines where constant-time operations matter.

## Key Concepts

- **Parity**: The XOR of all bits; 1 if an odd number of 1s, 0 if even.
- **XOR folding**: Successive shift+XOR operations collapse bit ranges without branches or loops.
- **128-bit handling**: Splitting into hi/lo halves and recombining.

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you adapt `parity128` to a 256-bit type?
- What is the time and space complexity of this approach?
- How would you make this constant-time for security-sensitive applications?

questions.question: In a 32-bit unsigned integer context, implement a rotate-left function rotl32(n, k) that shifts n left by k bits and wraps the high bits around to the low end. Do not use loops or conditionals. Provide the signature, a brief justification, and 1-2 concrete examples?

options.text: Compute distance128(a,b) by XORing words and running a two-word SWAR popcount on hi and lo, summing the results. Signature: unsigned distance128(uint128 a, uint128 b); no loops or conditional branches

Apply a 64-bit SWAR popcount to each half, then add. For a 64-bit v: v = v - ((v>>1)&0x5555555555555555ULL); v = (v & 0x3333333333333333ULL) + ((v>>2)&0x3333333333333333ULL); v = (v + (v>>4)) & 0x0F0F

uint32_t rotl32(uint32_t n, unsigned int k) { return (n << k) | (n >> ((32 - k) & 31)); } Rationale: Pure bitwise operations with a masked right shift ensure no undefined shifts when k=0. Examples: rotl32(0x12345678, 4)…

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests understanding of bitwise rotation, edge-case safety, and avoiding branches.

## Key Concepts
- Bitwise shifts and OR to wrap bits
- Masking to avoid undefined behavior with 0
- Avoiding loops/conditionals in low-level operations

## Code Example
```c
uint32_t rotl32(uint32_t n, unsigned int k) {
  return (n << k) | (n >> ((32 - k) & 31));
}
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you generalize to 64-bit width?
- How would you test boundary cases k=0 and k=31?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

This question tests precise bit-level reasoning and avoids expensive operations in tight loops, useful in storage engines and query optimizers.

## Key Concepts

- Single-set-bit test: (n & (n-1)) == 0
- Even-index bit mask: 0x55555555
- Combining tests with boolean logic for a robust check

## Code Example

```javascript
function isSingleEvenBitSet(n) {
  return n !== 0 && (n & (n - 1)) === 0 && (n & 0x55555555) !== 0;
}
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you generalize to check exactly k bits set?
- How would you adapt to 64-bit width and different bit-ordering considerations?

Use only bitwise operations and shifts; no loops or conditionals. Provide the function signature and a brief justification, plus 1-2 quick examples?

questions.question: Implement rotl64(uint64_t x, unsigned n) that rotates x left by n bits, using only shifts and masks; no loops or conditionals. Signature: uint64_t rotl64(uint64_t x, unsigned n); Provide signature, brief justification, and 1–2 quick tests?

options.text: Rotate left by using s = n & 63; return (x << s) | (x >> ((64 - s) & 63)); Handles s=0 safely and works with 64-bit wrap. Tests: rotl64(0x0123456789ABCDEFULL, 8) == 0x23456789ABCDEF01ULL; rotl64(1,63)

Sample approach: if x.hi != 0 then return __builtin_clzll(x.hi); else return 64 + __builtin_clzll(x.lo); This yields 0..128, with 128 for x==0. A portable version uses a LUT-based clz for 64-bit and a

Answer: MirrorBytes128 reverses the 16 bytes: y.hi = reverseBytes64(x.lo) and y.lo = reverseBytes64(x.hi). A branchless 64-bit reverse uses a fixed sequence of masks/shifts. Tests: input x.lo=0x112233
Approach: compute (x & (x >> 1)) != 0; return 1 if true, 0 otherwise. This checks every adjacent bit pair, including the boundary between the low and high halves. It uses only shifts and bitwise AND,

questions.question: In a bitmap pipeline, rotate a 32-bit mask left by an offset k (0–31) using only bitwise shifts and masks, no branches. Implement rotateLeft32(uint32_t x, unsigned k) returning the rotated value. Signature: uint32_t rotateLeft32(uint32_t x, unsigned k); Provide the signature, a brief justification, and 1–2 quick tests?

options.text: Proposed approach: implement packBits64 using a fixed, unrolled PDEP-like sequence with SWAR steps (1,2,4,8,16,32). Each step masks a block, shifts it toward the next available bit, and ORs into the r

rotateLeft32 performs a 32-bit rotate left by k (0–31) with no branches. Signature: uint32_t rotateLeft32(uint32_t x, unsigned k); Implementation: k &= 31; return (x << k) | (x >> ((32 - k) & 31)); Te
Branchless 256-bit trailing zeros via a four-word reduction. Determine the first nonzero word among w0..w3 using masks, accumulate an offset of 64 per zero word, then apply a 64-bit ctz on the chosen

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This tests precise bit manipulations without branches, a common need in codecs and bitboards.

## Key Concepts
- Bitwise shifts and masks
- Rotation vs simple shift
- Handling 0-31 safely without undefined behavior

## Code Example
```c
uint32_t rotateLeft32(uint32_t x, unsigned k){
    k &= 31;
    return (x << k) | (x >> ((32 - k) & 31));
}
```

## Follow-up Questions
- What happens for k=0 or k=31? Explain results and edge handling.
- How to generalize to 64-bit values? Discuss performance trade-offs and compiler optimizations.

questions.question: Implement compressBits128(uint128 x) that returns a uint64_t with the even-position bits of x (bits 0,2,...,126) packed into the lower 64 bits. Use only bitwise operations and shifts; no loops or conditionals. Signature: uint64_t compressBits128(uint128 x); Provide the signature, a brief justification, and 1–2 quick tests?

options.text: Strategy: fold hi and lo with a cascade of masks (0x5555555555555555), shifting by 1,2,4,8,16 to collapse every other bit, then OR and extract the lower 64 bits. This yields bit i of result = bit 2i o
Use a de Bruijn sequence to map the least-significant 1 to its trailing-zero count. If n==0 return 32. Let v = n & -n; idx = (v * 0x077CB531) >> 27; tz = debruijn[idx]. This uses only bitwise ops and

Approach: in each byte, preserve the MSB if that byte has any 1s. Do t = x; t |= t>>1; t |= t>>2; t |= t>>4; then mask with MSB per-byte 0x80...80. Return t & MSB_MASK. Signature: uint128 perByteMSB12

Approach: Treat the 128-bit value as x = hi:lo. Compute x<<1 across 128 bits: lo_shift = lo << 1; hi_shift = (hi << 1) | (lo >> 63). The number of 1-runs equals popcount(lo & ~lo_shift) + popcount(hi & ~hi_shift).

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
- Exercises branchless bitpacking across 128-bit inputs, a common primitive in hashing, CRC, or SIMD pre-processing.
- Emphasizes correctness guarantees and predictable performance.

## Key Concepts
- Layered masking to perform in-register bit packing.
- Cross-half (lo/hi) merging without branches.
- Endianness-agnostic interpretation of bit indices.

## Code Example
```javascript
// Signature and approach sketch
uint64_t compressBits128(uint128 x) {
  // Implementation uses cascade masks and shifts to pack even bits
  // into the lower 64 bits without loops or branches.
}
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you generalize this to 256-bit inputs?
- What are performance implications on different architectures (CPU vector units, compilers)?

questions.question: Implement hasConsecutiveZeros(uint32_t x) that returns a nonzero value if there exists at least two adjacent zero bits in the 32-bit representation of x, otherwise returns 0. Use only bitwise operations and shifts; no loops or conditionals. Signature: uint32_t hasConsecutiveZeros(uint32_t x); Provide the signature, a brief justification, and 1–2 quick tests?

options.text: Return (n >> k) | (n << ((64 - k) & 63)); with k masked by 63 to avoid undefined shifts when k=0. This performs a circular shift: bits shifted out on the right wrap to the left. Example: n=0x012345678
Signature: uint32_t hasConsecutiveZeros(uint32_t x); Approach: let y = ~x. A pair of adjacent zeros in x yields a 11 in y, detectable by t = y & (y >> 1). If t != 0, there is at least one consecutive

Approach: Split the 128-bit value into its hi and lo 64-bit halves. Apply the classic SWAR popcount algorithm to each half using bitwise reduction with masks 0x5555555555555555ULL, 0x3333333333333333ULL, and 0x0F0F0F0F0…

Use k = k & 31; return ((n << k) | (n >>> ((-k) & 31))) >>> 0; This rotates within 32 bits and handles k=0 without undefined shifts. Example: rotateLeft32(0x12345678, 4) -> 0x23456781; rotateLeft32(0x

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This tests ability to craft a compact bitwise predicate and reason about complements and bit patterns.

## Key Concepts
- Bitwise NOT
- Right shift and masking
- Pattern detection with parallel bit tricks

## Code Example

```c
uint32_t hasConsecutiveZeros(uint32_t x) {
  uint32_t y = ~x;
  return y & (y >> 1);
}
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How many such pairs can exist in a 32-bit word?
- How to adapt to 64-bit?

questions.question: Implement a 32-bit header packer. Write a function packHeader(uint8_t version, uint8_t flags, uint16_t length) that places version in bits 31-28, flags in bits 27-20, and length in bits 19-0. Use only bitwise operations and shifts, no loops or conditionals. Provide the signature and a brief justification, plus 1-2 quick examples?

options.text: A robust approach uses SWAR on each 64-bit half to count bits, then sums the halves. Count 4-bit nibbles with masks and shifts, fold to 8-, 16-, then add hi and lo. No branches; pure bit-twiddling ari

Approach: fold x using XOR shifts to reduce to a small value, then map the 4-bit result to a parity bit in constant time without branches. Signature: uint32 parity32(uint32 x); Tests: parity32(0)=0; p

header = ((version & 0x0F) << 28) | ((flags & 0xFF) << 20) | (length & 0xFFFFF); This masks each field to its width and packs them into a 32-bit word. Examples: packHeader(0xA,0x5A,0xFFFFF) -> 0xA5AFF

To swap adjacent bytes in a 32-bit value without loops, isolate the two byte pairs with masks 0x00FF00FF and 0xFF00FF00, shift them by 8 bits in opposite directions, and combine. Example: 0xAABBCCDD -

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests the ability to pack multiple fields into a single word, a common task in serialization and network protocols. It emphasizes precise bit-width handling and avoiding branches.

## Key Concepts

- Bitwise masking
- Bit shifts
- Field packing into a 32-bit word

## Code Example

```javascript
function packHeader(version, flags, length) {
  return ((version & 0x0F) << 28) | ((flags & 0xFF) << 20) | (length & 0xFFFFF);
}
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you extract version, flags, and length from the header?
- How would you validate inputs to prevent overflow or sign issues in languages with signed integers?

questions.question: Implement hasAdjacentOnes32(uint32_t x) that returns true if x contains at least one pair of adjacent 1 bits (e.g., 0b11 in any position). Use only bitwise shifts and masks; no loops or conditionals. Signature: bool hasAdjacentOnes32(uint32_t x); Provide the signature, a brief justification, and 1–2 quick tests?

options.text: Approach: compute z = x & (x >> 1); return z != 0; This branchless test detects any adjacent 1s in the 32-bit word in constant time. Tests: hasAdjacentOnes32(0x5) -> false; hasAdjacentOnes32(0b11) ->

Use a constant-time parallel prefix across 128 bits: propagate runs by masking and shifts, merge across 64-bit boundary, and extract the maximum. The result stores the max run length in the low 8 bits

rotl32(n,k) = (n << k) | (n >> (32 - k)); This is valid for 0 <= k < 32; uses two shifts and an OR, wrapping around high bits. Examples: rotl32(0x80000001, 1) = 0x00000003; rotl32(0x12345678, 4) = 0x23456781

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests understanding of efficient, branchless bit tricks and how to detect patterns across bit ranges.

## Key Concepts
- Branchless computation
- Bitwise AND and right shift
- Pattern detection in fixed width integers

## Code Example
```javascript
function hasAdjacentOnes32(x) {
  return (x & (x >>> 1)) !== 0;
}
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you adapt for a generic width W? 
- How would you test edge cases (all zeros, all ones, alternating patterns) and discuss performance implications?
